mnemonics.title: Pipeline Stages
mnemonics.content: IF ID EX MEM WB - INSTRUCTION FETCHES DELIVER EXCELLENT MEMORY WRITING BACK

mnemonics.title: Hazard Types
mnemonics.content: DATA HAZARDS: RAW WAR WAW - READ AFTER WRITE, WRITE AFTER READ, WRITE AFTER WRITE

mnemonics.title: Pipeline Performance Formula
mnemonics.content: IDEAL CPI = 1, ACTUAL CPI = IDEAL CPI + STALL CYCLES PER INSTRUCTION

acronyms.usage: First stage where instruction is fetched from memory
Second stage where instruction is decoded and registers are read
Third stage where ALU performs the operation
Fourth stage for memory read or write operations
Fifth stage where result is written back to register file
Degree to which instructions can be executed in parallel
Average clock cycles needed per instruction

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a car assembly line where each station performs one specific task (install engine, add wheels, paint, etc.). At any time, different cars are at different stations being worked on simultaneously.
Think of bubbles as empty spaces on a conveyor belt where no product moves forward, representing wasted time slots in the pipeline.
Structural hazards are like road construction blocking a lane; data hazards are like one car waiting for another to finish a task; control hazards are like uncertain directions causing wrong turns.
Picture a factory output: latency is how long ONE item takes, throughput is how many items come out per hour. Pipeline increases throughput, not latency.

analogies.analogy: A pipeline is like a fast-food restaurant where taking order, cooking, and serving happen at different counters. While one customer receives food, another is being served, and a third is placing order.

Hazards are like traffic problems: structural hazard is a one-lane road where cars must wait; data hazard is when you need information from someone who has not finished their task; control hazard is taking a wrong turn because you did not know the destination.
Pipeline flush is like hitting the emergency stop button on an assembly line when something goes wrong, clearing all items in progress.
The clock cycle is like the beat of a drum in an orchestra - all musicians (stages) must complete their part between beats to stay synchronized.
